inline_review:
commit 28b0b7481fb2c279e017e6caab4d78921976f5db
Author: Rakie Kim <rakie.kim@sk.com>

mm/mempolicy: enhance weighted interleave with socket-aware locality

This commit restricts weighted interleave allocations to the current socket
to reduce cross-socket traffic. If no eligible local nodes remain, it
attempts to fall back to other nodes in the policy.

> This patch makes weighted interleave socket-aware. Before weighting is
> applied, the candidate nodes are restricted to the current socket; only
> if no eligible local nodes remain does the policy fall back to the wider
> set.

Does the implementation actually fall back to the wider set?

Looking at policy_resolve_package_nodes(), it appears to fall back only to
the package of the first node in the policy:

> diff --git a/mm/mempolicy.c b/mm/mempolicy.c
> index a3f0fde6c626..541853ac08bc 100644
> --- a/mm/mempolicy.c
> +++ b/mm/mempolicy.c

[ ... ]

> +static int policy_resolve_package_nodes(struct mempolicy *policy, nodemask_t *mask)
> +{
> +	unsigned int node, ret = 0;
> +	nodemask_t package_mask;
> +
> +	if (!policy || !mask)
> +		return -EINVAL;
> +
> +	nodes_clear(*mask);
> +
> +	node = numa_node_id();
> +	ret = mp_get_package_nodes(node, &package_mask);

Can this call to mp_get_package_nodes() race with memory hot-remove?

If a concurrent memory hot-remove invokes destroy_package_node(), it acquires
a mutex, sets mpns[nid] = NULL, and immediately calls kfree(mpn).

Since mp_get_package_nodes() accesses the global mpns array locklessly, would
this result in a use-after-free?

Additionally, mp_get_package_nodes() internally calls package_node_is_valid(),
which contains unconditional pr_err() statements for uninitialized or missing
packages.

Could invoking this in the memory allocation fast path cause a severe log spam
if tasks fall back to a missing node package?

> +	if (!ret) {
> +		nodes_and(*mask, package_mask, policy->nodes);

Is it safe to read policy->nodes multiple times without stabilization?

If a concurrent set_mempolicy() or cpuset update modifies the policy, these
reads might observe intermediate or torn states.

> +
> +		if (nodes_empty(*mask)) {
> +			node = first_node(policy->nodes);
> +			ret = mp_get_package_nodes(node, &package_mask);
> +			if (ret)
> +				goto out;
> +			nodes_and(*mask, package_mask, policy->nodes);
> +			if (nodes_empty(*mask))
> +				goto out;
> +		}
> +	}

If torn reads occur across first_node() and the subsequent nodes_and(), could
this result in a completely empty mask? 

When the mask is empty, next_node_in() would return MAX_NUMNODES. Since the
page allocator then invokes __alloc_pages_node(MAX_NUMNODES), which accesses
NODE_DATA(MAX_NUMNODES), would this trigger an out-of-bounds array read and a
kernel panic?

Also, returning to the commit message's claim about falling back to the wider
set, this block seems to silently truncate the mask to only the first socket. 

If a user explicitly creates an MPOL_WEIGHTED_INTERLEAVE policy spanning
multiple remote sockets (e.g., nodes {2, 3} on separate packages) to aggregate
memory bandwidth, wouldn't this ignore the rest of the requested nodes and
break the UAPI semantics of the interleave policy?
output:
{"findings":[{"problem":"Logic flaw and API contract violation in `policy_resolve_package_nodes` fallback logic.","severity":"High","severity_explanation":"The commit message claims that if no eligible local nodes remain, the policy falls back to the "wider set" (the user's full policy). However, the implementation actually falls back to the package of the first node in the policy: `node = first_node(policy->nodes); ret = mp_get_package_nodes(node, ...); nodes_and(*mask, package_mask, policy->nodes);`. If a user explicitly creates an `MPOL_WEIGHTED_INTERLEAVE` policy spanning multiple remote sockets (e.g., nodes `{2, 3}` on separate packages) to aggregate memory bandwidth, this code silently truncates the mask to only the first socket (`{2}`). This ignores the rest of the requested nodes, breaking the fundamental UAPI semantics of the interleave policy and contradicting the stated intent."},{"problem":"Data race causing torn nodemask reads, leading to an out-of-bounds array access and kernel panic.","severity":"Critical","severity_explanation":"`policy_resolve_package_nodes()` reads the shared `policy->nodes` bitmask multiple times without any stabilization (e.g., in `nodes_and` and `first_node`). If a concurrent `set_mempolicy()` or cpuset update modifies the policy, these reads can observe intermediate/torn states. For instance, `first_node()` could return a node from the old mask, and the subsequent `nodes_and()` could intersect its package with the new mask, resulting in a completely empty mask. When the mask is empty, `next_node_in()` returns `MAX_NUMNODES`. The page allocator then invokes `__alloc_pages_node(MAX_NUMNODES)`, which accesses `NODE_DATA(MAX_NUMNODES)`. Since the `node_data` array is sized `MAX_NUMNODES`, this triggers an out-of-bounds array read and a kernel panic. A local user can trigger this by racing `mbind()` with concurrent memory allocations."},{"problem":"Use-After-Free and Denial of Service (Log Spam) vulnerabilities in the page allocation fast path.","severity":"Critical","severity_explanation":"`policy_resolve_package_nodes()` calls `mp_get_package_nodes()`, which locklessly accesses the global `mpns` array. Concurrently, a memory hot-remove operation can invoke `destroy_package_node()`, which acquires a mutex, sets `mpns[nid] = NULL`, and immediately calls `kfree(mpn)`. If an allocation races with this hot-remove, it will dereference the freed `mpns[nid]` pointer, resulting in a Use-After-Free. Furthermore, `mp_get_package_nodes()` internally calls `package_node_is_valid()`, which contains unconditional `pr_err()` statements for uninitialized or missing packages. Invoking this in the memory allocation fast path means that any task falling back to a missing node package will spam the kernel ring buffer millions of times per second, causing a severe Denial of Service."}],"fixes":"","review_inline":"commit 28b0b7481fb2c279e017e6caab4d78921976f5db
